macro_segments.title: The Ring of Idolatry Mixed with Permissible Rings and the Principle of Assumption When One Falls Away

micro_segments.title: The Case: Idolatrous Ring Mixed with One Hundred Permissible Rings, One Falls into the Sea
Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Blame the Lost Item and Assume the Remaining Are Permissible
Rabbah's Challenge to Rav Nachman: Why Not Apply This to the Mishna's Case of Animals?
Rabbi Elazar's Limitation: Rabbi Eliezer Only Permits When Two Items Are Used at a Time

macro_segments.title: Mixture of Idolatrous Ring with One Hundred Rings, Then Split into Forty and Sixty, With a Ring from the Minority Group Falling into Another Mixture

micro_segments.title: The Setup: Forty Rings and Sixty Rings from Original Mixture, One from the Forty Falls into a New Mixture
Standard Rule: One Ring from Forty Does Not Forbid the New Mixture; One Ring from Sixty Does
Gemara's Question: Why Not Apply the Principle of 'What Separates from the Majority Separates' in Both Cases?
Resolution: The Distinction Rests on What Happens When the Entire Minority or Majority Group Separates Off

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Position on When Bittul Applies to Idolatry Across Multiple Mixtures versus Other Prohibitions

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Statement: By Idolatry Specifically, Even One Item Never Undergoes Bittul, No Matter How Many Mixtures
Baraita of Goblets: A Double Doubt (Sfek Sfeika) by Idolatry Becomes Permissible at the Third Mixture
Tannaitic Debate: Rabbi Yehuda (Never Batel) versus Rabbi Shimon (Batel by Third Mixture) Applied to Orlah Pomegranates
Analysis: Reconciling Shmuel with the Baraita—Shmuel Splits the Vote Between Idolatry and Other Prohibitions

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish and Rava on Blaming a Lost Item When a Sealed Barrel of Terumah Falls into the Sea

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Case: A Sealed Barrel of Terumah Mixed with One Hundred Other Barrels, One Falls into the Sea
Why Both the Ring and Barrel Cases Are Permitted: Extending the Principle Beyond Idolatry to Terumah
Rava's Limitation: Only by a Barrel Where the Loss Is Perceptible; Not by a Fig That Falls into Other Figs
Rav Yosef's Counterargument: Even a Fig, Like a Barrel, Has Its Absence Noticed and Can Be Blamed

macro_segments.title: Rav Elazar's Solution: Opening One of the Sealed Barrels and Removing a Proportional Amount of Wine to Permit All

micro_segments.title: The Method: Open One Barrel and Remove One-Hundredth of Its Contents to Account for the Mixed Prohibition
Rav Nachman's Objection: This Appears to be Actively Causing Bittul Le-Chatchila, Which Is Forbidden
Correction: Only if One Barrel Opened Naturally; Otherwise It Violates the Rule Against Nullifying Prohibition Intentionally
Rabbi Oshaya's Extension: When Multiple Sealed Barrels Open, Remove the Appropriate Proportion from Each and Permit Them

topical_tags.term: בטל (Batel/Bittul) — Nullification of Prohibited Items in a Mixture
עבודה זרה (Avoda Zara) — Idolatry and Items Used for Idolatrous Worship
Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Blame It on the One That Was Lost/Brought
כל דפריש מרובא פריש (Kol De-Parish Me-Rubba Parish) — What Separates from the Majority Separates
שמואל (Shmuel) — Amoraic Position on Idolatry and Bittul Across Multiple Mixtures
Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Shimon Debate: Prohibition Never Batel versus Batel at Third Mixture
ספק ספיקה (Sfek Sfeika) — Double Doubt, Permissible in Certain Contexts
תרומה (Terumah) — Priestly Portion, Subject to Different Leniencies Than Idolatry
ערלה (Orlah) — Fruit from Young Trees, Not Nullified in Mixture
Rav Nachman — Transmits Rav's Position on the Ring Case
Reish Lakish — Permits Blaming the Lost Barrel, Conditions on Perceptibility
Rava — Limits Reish Lakish's Rule to Barrels Where Loss Is Noticeable, Not Small Items
Rav Yosef — Extends Permission Even to Small Items Like Figs
Rav Elazar — Proposes Opening Sealed Barrels and Removing Proportional Wine
אין מבטלין איסור לכתחילה (Ein Mevatlin Issur Le-Chatchila) — One Cannot Intentionally Nullify a Prohibition
Sealed Barrels (חביות סגורות) — Not Subject to Bittul, Remain Significant
Rabbi Oshaya — Extension of the Barrel Solution to Cases of Multiple Openings
